preamble: The transformation of the voiceless velar plosive /k/ to the voiced velar plosive /g/ is a common phonetic shift, primarily driven by processes of voicing assimilation or lenition. This change often occurs in specific phonological environments, such as intervocalically, after nasals, or in morphologically conditioned contexts.

examples.note: Word-initial /k/ shifted to /g/ in the transition to Western Turkish.
Consonant alternation (yumuşama) where word-final /k/ voices to /ğ/ (often realized as [g] or [ɣ]) when a vowel-initial suffix is added.
Lenition voices intervocalic /k/ to /g/ as part of broader Romance phonological changes.
Rendaku, an assimilatory process where the initial /k/ of the second element in a compound voices to /g/ after a vowel.
The change of /k/ to /g/ occurs in certain environments in Kikuyu.

sources: Hayes, B. (2018). Class 10, 5/2/2018: Paradigm Uniformity I A LITTLE BIT ON HIDDEN STRUCTURE.
Journal Uniku. (2025). CONTRASTIVE ANALYSIS OF PHONEME CHANGES IN JAPANESE AND SUNDANESE REDUPLICATION.
Meinhof, C., & van Warmelo, N. J. (1932). Introduction to the Phonology of the Bantu Languages. Dietrich Reimer Verlag.
